info.name: Setu Reconciliation Service
info.description:
Payment event ingestion and reconciliation.

**Run it top to bottom** — the requests are ordered as a story, not an index. Folder 2 proves idempotency by replaying an event and showing the count doesn't move; folder 4 walks the discrepancy classes.

Most requests carry test assertions, so `Run collection` is a real end-to-end check rather than a click-through.

### Setup

One variable: `base_url`.

- Live demo (default): `https://setu-recon.onrender.com`
- Local: `http://localhost:8000`

⏳ **The live demo sleeps after 15 min idle.** The first request may take 30–50s while Render wakes it. Not broken — retry once.

### Where the numbers come from

The service is loaded with the provided `sample_events.json`: **10,355 events → 10,165 stored** (190 duplicates absorbed), 3,800 transactions, 5 merchants.

variable.description: Live demo. Swap to http://localhost:8000 to run against docker compose.
An event already ingested from sample_events.json — used to demonstrate idempotency.
A real double settlement — two 'settled' events, different event_ids, 4 minutes apart.

request.description:
Deliberately does NOT touch the database.

A liveness probe that queried MySQL would report the app as dead during a transient DB blip and invite the orchestrator to restart a process that is fine. Liveness answers 'is this process wedged?'; readiness answers 'can it serve?'.

item.name: Readiness — the idempotency proof, visible from outside

request.description:
Checks the database AND reports row counts, so you can confirm the sample data is loaded rather than finding an empty database behind a healthy-looking service.

**events: 10165 from a 10,355-event file.** The 190 missing are duplicates the primary key refused. That gap is the whole design.

exec: // 10,165 is the baseline loaded from sample_events.json. It GROWS if you POST new
// events - that is the API working, not a failure. So assert the floor, not equality.
pm.test('at least the 10,165 loaded from sample_events.json', () => pm.expect(c.events).to.be.at.least(10165));
// The real claim: we stored FEWER than the 10,355 we were sent, because 190 were
// duplicates the primary key refused. This stays true no matter how much demo traffic runs.
pm.test('fewer stored than sent - 190 duplicates absorbed', () => pm.expect(c.events - 10165).to.be.below(190));
pm.test('5 merchants from the sample file', () => pm.expect(c.merchants).to.be.at.least(5));

item.name: 2. Idempotency — the core requirement
item.description: Replay an event that is already stored. It must be a no-op, and it must return 200.

request.description:
This event is already in the database (it is the first record in sample_events.json).

**Why 200 and not 409?** The caller's intent — 'this event should be on record' — is satisfied either way. Webhook senders retry on timeouts and 5xx; a 409 would tell a correctly-behaving client it did something wrong, and could trigger alerts or dead-lettering for a *successful* retry. Idempotency exists so retries are safe; erroring on a safe retry defeats it.

The per-event `status` still lets the caller observe the dedup.

**Send it as many times as you like.** The answer never changes.

exec: // Snapshot the event count so the next request can prove the replay moved it by zero,
// rather than comparing against a hardcoded number that demo traffic invalidates.
pm.test('200, NOT an error — a safe retry is not a failure', () => pm.response.to.have.status(200));

item.name: Count is unchanged — the replay wrote nothing

request.description: Still 10,165. The replay did not create a row, and did not touch the transaction's state.

exec: pm.test('replaying an event moved the count by EXACTLY zero', () => pm.expect(now).to.eql(before));

item.name: 3. Ingest a fresh transaction
item.description: Create a new transaction end to end, then replay it to prove idempotency on data created during this run rather than pre-loaded data.

request.description:
`POST /events` takes a single object **or** an array — one endpoint for the partner's live feed and their replay/backfill path, which differ only in cardinality.

The whole batch lands in one database transaction: every event with its projection updated, or none.

exec: // Fresh ids each run, so the collection is re-runnable.

item.name: Replay the SAME batch → all duplicates

request.description:
The same event twice, in one request — a duplicate **inside a single batch**, which is a different code path from two separate calls. The provided sample file contains 190 such pairs.

Both are duplicates here: one was already stored by the previous request, and the two in this body are identical to each other.

item.name: Fetch it — state + full event history

request.description:
Two events, not three — the replay added nothing.

Note `payment_status: processed` and `settlement_status: unsettled` as **independent axes**, and the complete event history: the audit trail that lets an operator answer *why* a transaction is in this state.

exec: pm.test('exactly 2 events — the replay added nothing', () => pm.expect(t.event_count).to.eql(2));

item.name: 4. Reconciliation — discrepancies
item.description: The five classes, and the two that are only findable because payment and settlement are separate axes.

request.description:
Expected against the sample data:

| type | count |
|---|---|
| processed_never_settled | 380 |
| stuck_pending | 190 |
| settled_despite_failure | 95 |
| duplicate_settlement | 95 |

**duplicate_settlement is 95, not 162.** Naive ingestion would report 162 — the other 67 are the same settlement event delivered twice. Idempotency isn't hygiene here: get it wrong and the report is inflated 70%, and an ops team chases 67 problems that don't exist.

A transaction can breach several rules, so `counts_by_type` may sum to more than `pagination.total`.

exec: // 380 is the sample-data baseline. It grows by one every time folder 3 runs:
// that demo transaction goes initiated -> processed and never settles, and it is
// dated well past the 24h SLA. So the count going up is the RULE WORKING on data
// this collection created - not a regression.
pm.test('at least the 380 processed-but-never-settled from the sample data',
// THE assertion. Exact, deliberately: naive ingestion reports 162 here, because 67
// of those are the same settlement event delivered twice. at.least(95) would pass
// on 162 and hide exactly the bug this proves we don't have.
pm.test('EXACTLY 95 duplicate settlements - not the 162 a naive implementation reports',

item.name: Money moved for a FAILED payment (95)

request.description:
The assignment's 'settlement recorded for a failed payment'.

This is **only representable** because payment_status and settlement_status are independent columns. With one combined `status` enum, `settled` would have overwritten `failed` — and these 95 transactions would be indistinguishable from the 2,470 healthy ones. You cannot query for what you have already erased.

exec: pm.test('each is failed AND settled at once', () => b.data.forEach(d => {

request.description:
Counts settlement **events**, not rows. A settlement replayed under the same event_id never reaches the counter — INSERT IGNORE drops it before the projection is recomputed.

So these 95 are real double settlements. The 67 that a naive implementation would also report are retries, and correctly absent.

exec: pm.test('each settled more than once', () => b.data.forEach(d => pm.expect(d.settled_event_count).to.be.above(1)));

request.description:
Two rules are **time-relative**: a transaction becomes a discrepancy purely by getting older, with no event to trigger a write.

That is why discrepancies are computed at read time and not stored at ingest — a saved `is_broken` flag would be wrong the instant the clock crossed the SLA, unless a cron job re-swept the table forever.

`?as_of=` also pins the evaluation to a fixed instant, so an ops report can be re-run later and produce the same numbers.

**Where 24h came from:** measured, not guessed. Every settlement in the sample data lands within 6.00h of processing (p99 = 5.95h); every never-settled transaction is ≥17.3h old. 24h sits in that gap with 4× margin, and matches T+1 settlement convention in Indian payments.

The exact predicate behind each class — the same strings the queries are built from, not a prose paraphrase that could drift.

Reconciliation output is only trustworthy if an operator can audit what the service means by 'broken'.

Counts alone aren't actionable. The money is:

| field | meaning |
|---|---|
| `expected_settlement_amount` | money that **should** have settled |
| `settled_amount` | money that **did** settle |
| `unreconciled_amount` | the gap ops has to chase |

**This endpoint and /discrepancies are independent SQL, and they agree exactly:**

```
unreconciled  =  processed_never_settled  −  settled_despite_failure
₹7,537,522.50 =  ₹9,636,198.03            −  ₹2,098,675.53
```

One GROUP BY with CASE aggregates computes all of it in a single pass. No Python arithmetic.

exec: pm.test('unreconciled = expected - settled, per group', () => b.groups.forEach(g => {

request.description:
Any combination of `merchant`, `date`, `status`, `payment_status`, `settlement_status`. One query serves every shape — the alternative is a hand-written query per combination, and three places for them to drift apart.

Dimensions resolve through a whitelist: GROUP BY takes an identifier, which cannot be a bound parameter, so user input is used to *look up* SQL we wrote rather than to *become* SQL.

`date_to` is **exclusive** — avoids the classic BETWEEN bug of silently dropping everything timestamped after 00:00:00.000 on the final day.
₹7.5M unreconciled across 3,800 transactions.

exec: pm.test('at least the 3,800 loaded from sample_events.json', () => pm.expect(pm.response.json().totals.transaction_count).to.be.at.least(3800));

item.name: Filter: failed payments that settled anyway

request.description: The two axes combine to express what a single flattened status cannot. This is the same 95 transactions the discrepancy endpoint finds, reached from the other direction.

item.name: Filter by merchant + date range, sorted by amount

request.description:
All filtering, sorting, counting and pagination happen in SQL. Python never receives a row it intends to discard.

`total` comes from `COUNT(*) OVER ()` — a window function evaluated after WHERE but before LIMIT, so one round trip returns both the page and the full count. A second COUNT query would double the round trips and could disagree with the page under concurrent writes.

exec: pm.test('all from the requested merchant', () => b.data.forEach(t => pm.expect(t.merchant.merchant_id).to.eql('merchant_3')));

request.description:
Offset pagination with a total count, because ops tooling wants 'page 4 of 380'.

ORDER BY always carries `transaction_id` as a tie-breaker. Without it, tied sort values may be permuted differently per query, so a row can appear on two pages while another is silently skipped — and nobody notices until an ops person swears a transaction vanished.

At 3,800 rows offset is free. Past ~100k it should become keyset pagination — named in the README rather than silently over-built for scale that doesn't exist.

exec: pm.test('total is the full count, not the page size', () => pm.expect(p.total).to.be.at.least(3800));

item.name: A real double settlement, with its audit trail

request.description:
StyleHub, ₹41,263.87. **Two 'settled' events with different event_ids, four minutes apart** — a genuine double settlement, not a retry.

The event history is the evidence. This is why payment_events is append-only: it is what lets an operator answer *why*, instead of guessing.

exec: pm.test('the two settlements have DIFFERENT event_ids — real, not a replay', () => pm.expect(s[0].event_id).to.not.eql(s[1].event_id));

item.name: 7. Validation and error handling
item.description: Every failure returns one envelope: {"error": {"code", "message", "field"}} — so a client writes one error handler, not one per endpoint.

request.description:
Every field is wrong. Pydantic rejects it before a single line of SQL runs.

That guarantee is why the query layer can be plain SQL with bound parameters and no defensive re-checking: everything downstream is guaranteed a well-formed UUID, a known event type, an exact Decimal and a timezone-aware instant.

Note the typo: `ammount`.

`extra="forbid"` — silently ignoring it would mean accepting an event whose amount we never read, and reconciling money we never received. A typo'd field name is a bug, not something to swallow.

ORDER BY takes an **identifier**, and identifiers cannot be bound as parameters — so this is the one place where SQL structure depends on user input.

It resolves through a whitelist dict: the input is used to *look up* SQL we wrote, never to *become* SQL. An unknown key raises before touching the database.

Everywhere else, values are bound parameters sent to MySQL on a separate channel from the query text, so a value can never be parsed as code.

exec: pm.test('rejected before reaching SQL (422 from our whitelist, or 403 from Render edge WAF)', () => pm.expect(pm.response.code).to.be.oneOf([422, 403]));

request.description: All 3,800 still there.
A valid UUID that doesn't exist. Same error envelope.

exec: pm.test('same envelope as every other error', () => pm.expect(pm.response.json().error.code).to.eql('not_found'));

request.description: Capped at 100, so a caller cannot turn a paginated endpoint into a full table scan.
